$schema: This is descriptive research evidence, not normative doctrine
note: All content extracted from research sources. This does NOT assign Prisma colors to films or create rankings. All film examples are illustrative only.

cinematographers.name: Roger Deakins
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: Pioneer of Digital Intermediate (DI) process and advocate for 'justified stylization' - even extreme color manipulation must serve narrative purpose and maintain photographic believability.
key_concepts: contradicting physical reality through digital tools
recurring_color_ideas: dried-out golden hues for period atmosphere
digital manipulation to achieve impossible natural looks
cited_films: O Brother, Where Art Thou? (2000) - first complete Digital Intermediate
The Aviator (2004) - replicating historical film stock looks
cinematographers.technical_approach: Utilizes DI process to digitally paint period atmospheres that contradict physical reality. Seeks sepia-toned, dust-bowl aesthetic through selective color manipulation.
source_citations: The Chromatic Turn: A Comprehensive Analysis of Color in Global Cinema

cinematographers.name: Robert Richardson
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: The 'Historical Chameleon' - intense collaborator who recreates specific historical film stocks through digital emulation. Views colorist as vital creative collaborator equal to gaffer or key grip.
key_concepts: pushing exposure to edge of burning out
cited_films: The Aviator (2004) - digitally emulated 2-strip and 3-strip Technicolor
Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood - matched digital to photochemical Ektachrome look
Films with Martin Scorsese and Quentin Tarantino
cinematographers.technical_approach: Meticulously matches digital dailies to historical film stock looks. Uses hot top-lighting and willingness to push exposure for psychological intensity.

cinematographers.name: Ellen Kuras
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: The 'Emotional Organicist' - uses organic approach with handheld work and practical lighting to create intimacy and memory destabilization.
recurring_color_ideas: color palette shifts reflecting memory erasure
vibrant reality to washed-out fragmented states
degradation of image mirroring character psychology
cited_films: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind - color shifts reflecting memory erasure
cinematographers.technical_approach: Handheld camerawork and practical lighting. Uses image degradation (fading light, focus shifts) to mirror character's internal state.
source_citations: Ellen Kuras interviews on organic cinematography

cinematographers.name: Bradford Young
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: Pivotal in reshaping how black skin is lit and colored in cinema. Critiques industry standard of over-lighting dark skin, advocates for underexposure and available light to render natural reflectivity.
key_concepts: visual dignity through physics of light
recurring_color_ideas: underexposure to respect skin's natural reflectivity
cinematographers.technical_approach: Favors underexposure, caps practical lamps, uses negative fill to carve out image from darkness. Challenges over-lighting of dark skin.
source_citations: Bradford Young interviews on lighting black skin

cinematographers.name: Vittorio Storaro
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: Legendary figure who treats color as philosophical and psychological language. Views cinematography as 'writing with light' with deep symbolic meaning.
recurring_color_ideas: red as life force and passion
color as emotional and spiritual expression
cinematographers.technical_approach: Uses color symbolically and philosophically. Treats each hue as having specific psychological and narrative meaning.

cinematographers.name: Christopher Doyle
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: Master of 'neon city' aesthetic and atmospheric color. Known for embracing available light and environmental color rather than imposing artificial schemes.
cinematographers.technical_approach: Embraces available artificial lighting, particularly neon and mixed urban sources. Uses atmospheric conditions as color elements.

cinematographers.name: Darius Khondji
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: Master of dark, atmospheric cinematography with distinctive use of underexposure and chemical processes to create mood and texture.
cinematographers.technical_approach: Uses underexposure and chemical processes to create distinctive atmospheric and textural effects.

cinematographers.name: Emmanuel Lubezki
cinematographers.philosophy_summary: Renowned for natural light mastery and seamless integration of color with environment and narrative flow.
cinematographers.technical_approach: Emphasizes natural light sources and environmental color integration for narrative flow.

extraction_notes.methodology: Content extracted directly from source document without interpretation or assignment to Prisma color categories
extraction_notes.limitations: Source document emphasizes Western cinematographers; global representation may be incomplete
extraction_notes.ambiguity_preservation: Where sources presented conflicting views on cinematographer approaches, both perspectives are maintained
